About Alian AI

Engineered in India. Shipping AI globally.
Alian AI is the AI services division of Alian Software— a 15+ year old engineering firm shipping production enterprise systems for clients across manufacturing, finance, and SaaS. We spun up the AI division to do one thing well: build production-ready AI that ships in weeks, backed by people who've shipped real software for a long time.

We're not a consultancy that built a deck about agents. We're an engineering team that ships them — chatbots, agents, RAG apps, automation pipelines, predictive models — and hands them over to your team to run.

15+

years of Alian Software engineering DNA

4

service pillars · agents, apps, strategy, data

$25

starting hourly rate · senior talent

4–8 wk

typical fixed-fee sprint timeline

How we work
We build, we don't pitch.
Every recommendation we make, we can also implement. That's why our scoping is honest and our timelines are real.

Senior engineers on every project.
No juniors learning on your dime. Backed by Alian Software's 15+ years of enterprise engineering bench.

You own the code, day one.
Code, prompts, agent configs, deployment artifacts — yours from commit one. No vendor lock-in disguised as 'our framework.'

Production-ready, not slide-ready.
Most engagements ship in 4–8 weeks. We measure ourselves on what's running in your stack, not on what's in a deck.

The team
The leadership that's run Alian Software for 15+ years now backs every Alian AI engagement. No account executives, no handoffs — you'll work directly with senior engineers from day one, and the people below stay accountable to the outcome.

Leadership
Mevil Bhojani is CEO



Clients are family. The best way to solve their problems is to understand their needs first.

Mehul Parmar is CFO



Sharp on numbers, sharper on commercial strategy. Finance built to accelerate the business, not slow it down.

Ravi Bhojani is CMO



Marketing earns trust by telling the truth about the work. No slogans, no hype — just the story of what we ship.

Nimesh Dixit is CTO



Production-grade code or it doesn't ship. Architecture the team can actually maintain — built to last past the demo.

Rashesh Makwana is Creative Director


Design that signals real expertise. The brand should look exactly like the team behind it — direct, considered, built to last.

Engineering & Design
The senior engineers and designers who actually ship the work. The people you'll meet on the discovery call — and the same ones still on the call at handover.

Harmit Mendapara

Fullstack AI Engineer & Software Architect

Ships agents and AI apps end-to-end. Architecture that holds up past the demo, code the team can actually maintain.

Jay Dara

Fullstack AI Engineer

11+ years of shipping production software, now focused on AI-powered features. Fast prototypes first, then the polish that turns them into product.

Shiv Joshi

Fullstack AI Engineer

Backend-rooted, fullstack-fluent. Builds the pipelines, embeddings, and integrations that turn AI demos into production features.

Hetal Makwana

Creative AI Designer

13+ years of product design, now focused on AI-first creative work. Translates complex models into experiences that feel obvious to use.

Want to see if we're a fit?
20 minutes. Honest scoping. We'll tell you upfront if we're not the right team.

Book a 20-min call →

# Tell us your problem. We'll architect the AI that solves it.

Every business has a workflow that's begging for AI. Type yours below — our agent will scope a solution in seconds. Then talk to a human to build it.

Our support team is buried in repetitive tickets

Solve it

Our AI scopes a solution in ~8 seconds. No signup required. ⌘/Ctrl + Enter to submit.

Engineered by the team behind Alian Software · 15+ years shipping enterprise systems

Live agent activity

- YOUTUBEPipeline generated 6 short-form variants for episode 1420s ago
- LEADLead qualification agent → routed inquiry to AE23s ago
- MANUFACTURINGProduction monitor flagged drift on machine 1411s ago
- SUPPORTAuto-resolution: ticket #4729 fixed and PR merged17s ago

How we work

## From "we have a problem" to live in production. The whole arc.

Most engagements ship in 4–6 weeks. Here's the timeline.

- Day 0

### Discovery call

20 min · We listen, we ask, we scope.
- Day 7

### Scope & SOW

Fixed price · No surprises.
- Week 4–6

### Build & weekly demos

Senior engineering team · Working code from day one.
- Week 6+

### Live & iterating

Your team owns it · We support if you want us to.

[Start at Day 0 →](/book-a-demo)

What we build

## Whatever the problem looks like, one of these is the solution.

- [### AI Agents & Automation

When your business needs hands that don't sleep.

Lead-qualification agents, support agents, ops automation. n8n, LangGraph, voice agents.

Learn more →](/services/agents-automation)
- [### Custom AI Apps & Chatbots

When your users need answers they can ask for.

RAG-powered chatbots, knowledge bases, custom LLM apps. Production-grade, branded, multitenant.

›

Learn more →](/services/apps-chatbots)
- [### AI Strategy & Enablement

When you need a plan before a build.

AI roadmap, governance, team training. For leaders figuring out where AI fits in the next 12 months.

Data readiness—

Use-case clarity—

Team enablement—

Learn more →](/services/strategy)
- [### Data & Intelligence

When your data is begging to be useful.

Predictive ML, computer vision, generative content pipelines. Turn data into decisions.

Learn more →](/services/data-intelligence)

Real builds

## Real problems. Real builds. Real numbers.

- Production
- Maintenance
- Quality
- Inventory
- Energy
- Briefing

ManufacturingAgents & Automation

### Multi-Agent System for 24/7 Manufacturing Plant

31%

Reduction in unplanned downtime · 90 days

Six specialized agents watching production, maintenance, quality, inventory, energy, and shift handoffs — coordinating through a shared event bus, alerting humans via WhatsApp.

- 8×

faster anomaly detection
- 0

stockouts (vs 7 prior)
- 9%

energy savings

[Read the full case study →](/work/multi-agent-manufacturing)

Why teams choose us

## Engineering rigor, agency speed, global pricing.

- ### Production-ready in 6 weeks.

We ship working code from week one — not slides, not theory.
- ### Backed by Alian Software's engineering bench.

15+ years of enterprise software DNA. No juniors on your project.
- ### Your data, your models, your IP.

Code transferred on day one. Prompts, agents, configs — all yours to own and extend.
- ### Globally competitive rates · starting at $25/hr.

Engineered in India, shipping globally. Same senior talent at a fraction of the SF rate.

What clients say

## Three quotes from the people writing the renewal checks.

- > "I expected a fancy dashboard. What we got was a team of agents that quietly do the watching so my humans can focus on doing."

Plant Operations Manager

Manufacturing client

Multi-agent operations system · 31% downtime reduction
- > "The first time I saw a Claude-Code-authored PR ship to production with no human edits, I was nervous. 90 days later I think about it the same way I think about any other contributor."

VP of Engineering

B2B SaaS · DevTools

Auto-resolution chatbot + Claude Code
- > "The agent qualifies better than my junior SDRs because it never has a bad day. And when it doesn't know, it knows it doesn't know — and routes to a human."

VP of Sales

B2B SaaS

Lead qualification agent · 47% more demos

1\",\"h\",{\"children\":[null,[\"$\",\"$La\",null,{\"children\":\"$Lb\"}],[\"$\",\"div\",null,{\"hidden\":true,\"children\":[\"$\",\"$Lc\",null,{\"children\":[\"$\",\"$7\",null,{\"name\":\"Next.Metadata\",\"children\":\"$Ld\"}]}]}],[\"$\",\"meta\",null,{\"name\":\"next-size-adjust\",\"content\":\"\"}]]}],false]],\"m\":\"$undefined\",\"G\":[\"$e\",[]],\"S\":true,\"h\":null,\"s\":\"$undefined\",\"l\":\"$undefined\",\"p\":\"$undefined\",\"d\":\"$undefined\",\"b\":\"KVhZt3bMsqpR7hikm1mgQ\"}\n"])self.\_\_next\_f.push([1,"f:[]\n9:\"$Wf\"\n"])self.\_\_next\_f.push([1,"10:I[59829,[\"/\_next/static/chunks/0mx87by.4ar12.js\",\"/\_next/static/chunks/0~zccwm3lhu\_r.js\",\"/\_next/static/chunks/0v0bp2xj48y9l.js\",\"/\_next/static/chunks/17sbd3ocho8it.js\",\"/\_next/static/chunks/05cuwq4vzvm4q.js\",\"/\_next/static/chunks/0or~xwe\_b~jln.js\",\"/\_next/static/chunks/0-5n9rnmsahxg.js\",\"/\_next/static/chunks/08b2kcty4\_6fl.js\"],\"BlogIndex\"]\n11:T277d,"])self.\_\_next\_f.push([1,"# The quality complaint you keep getting\n\nYour RAG feature shipped. Users are complaining that answers are wrong, incomplete, or confidently off-topic. You've already tried the obvious things: larger chunks, smaller chunks, better prompts. Someone on the team suggested swapping the embedding model. Another person wants to double the top-k and stuff more context into the prompt.\n\nBoth of those paths cost real engineering time and real token spend. Neither one fixes the core problem: \*\*your retriever is returning plausible chunks, not the most relevant ones.\*\* The ranking is wrong. And the LLM can't save you from a bad retrieval list.\n\nReranking fixes the ranking. It's a one-day change in most stacks. It consistently improves answer quality more than embedding swaps do, costs less to operate than inflating top-k, and it's the first thing we add to any RAG system that's underperforming in production.\n\nThis post gives you the before/after architecture, the model options with honest tradeoffs, and a decision table for when reranking earns its latency cost — and when it doesn't.\n\n---\n\n# What actually goes wrong in standard RAG retrieval\n\nA standard RAG pipeline does this:\n\n- Embed the user query into a vector\n- Run approximate nearest-neighbor search over your chunk index\n- Take the top-k chunks (usually 5–20) by cosine similarity\n- Stuff them into the prompt in retrieval order\n- Hope the LLM figures out which ones are actually relevant\n\nThe problem is that embedding similarity is a \*\*blunt instrument\*\*. It's optimized for semantic relatedness, not for answer-relevance to a specific query. Two chunks can be highly similar in embedding space but wildly different in how useful they are for answering the exact question asked.\n\nThe result: your top-k is noisy. The LLM sees chunk 3 (marginally relevant) ranked above chunk 7 (the one that actually answers the question). Research on LLM context use is consistent — models weight early context more heavily than late context. Wrong ranking means wrong answers, even when the right chunk is technically in the window.\n\nSwapping embedding models helps a little. Smaller chunks help a little. Neither solves the fundamental problem that similarity ≠ relevance.\n\n---\n\n# The fix: a two-stage retrieval architecture\n\nThe architecture change is simple:\n\n- \*\*Stage 1 — Recall:\*\* Use your existing vector search (or hybrid search with BM25) to pull a broad candidate set. Top-20 to top-50 chunks. This stage is optimized for recall, not precision.\n- \*\*Stage 2 — Rerank:\*\* Pass the query + every candidate chunk to a reranker model. The reranker scores each (query, chunk) pair independently and returns a relevance score. Sort by that score, take the top-3 to top-5.\n- \*\*Stage 3 — Generate:\*\* Send only the reranked top chunks to the LLM.\n\nYou're separating two jobs that your current system conflates: finding candidates and judging relevance. The vector index is fast and cheap but imprecise. The reranker is slower and slightly more expensive but far more precise.\n\nThe net effect: you send fewer, better chunks to the LLM. Token cost often goes down. Answer quality goes up. Hallucinations tied to irrelevant context drop noticeably.\n\n---\n\n# Model choices: cross-encoders, Cohere Rerank, ColBERT\n\nNot all rerankers are the same. Here's the honest breakdown.\n\n## Cross-encoder models (self-hosted)\n\nCross-encoders read the query and the chunk together in a single forward pass, which is why they're more accurate than embedding similarity — they can see the interaction between the two.\n\n- Good open-source options: `ms-marco-MiniLM-L-6-v2`, `bge-reranker-large` (BAAI), `cross-encoder/ms-marco-electra-base`\n- Deploy on a small GPU instance or CPU if your candidate set is under ~50 chunks\n- Latency: roughly 50–150ms for 20 candidates on CPU, 20–40ms on GPU\n- Cost: essentially free after infrastructure, no per-call API fees\n- \*\*Best for:\*\* teams with data-residency requirements, high query volume, or a need to fine-tune on domain-specific data\n\n## Cohere Rerank (API)\n\nCohere's hosted reranker is the fastest path to production. One API call, returns relevance scores, done.\n\n- `rerank-english-v3.0` and `rerank-multilingual-v3.0` are the current production models\n- Pricing is per 1,000 search units (query + document pair), so costs scale with candidate set size and query volume\n- Latency adds roughly 200–400ms to the retrieval leg depending on candidate count\n- No GPU infra to manage, no model deployment\n- \*\*Best for:\*\* teams moving fast, lower query volumes, or those who want to validate reranking ROI before committing to self-hosted infra\n\n## ColBERT / late interaction models\n\nColBERT sits between dense retrieval and full cross-encoding. It pre-computes token-level embeddings for documents and does a late interaction at query time, which makes it faster than a cross-encoder at scale.\n\n- `colbert-ir/colbertv2.0` is the reference model; RAGatouille is the easiest Python wrapper\n- Better throughput than cross-encoders at large candidate sets (100+)\n- More complex to deploy and index than either of the above options\n- \*\*Best for:\*\* large corpora (1M+ chunks), teams already running vector search at scale, or cases where cross-encoder latency is unacceptable\n\n---\n\n# When reranking earns its latency cost — and when it doesn't\n\nReranking adds latency. That's real. Here's how to think about whether the trade is worth it.\n\n\*\*Reranking is clearly worth it when:\*\*\n\n- Your retrieval corpus has more than ~5,000 chunks — the noisier the candidate set, the more a reranker helps\n- Queries are longer or more complex than single keywords — cross-encoders shine on natural language questions\n- Wrong answers have meaningful consequences (support bots giving bad policy information, internal tools surfacing stale compliance docs)\n- Your current top-k is 10 or more — you're already paying token cost for chunks that aren't helping\n- You've measured that answer quality is the bottleneck, not retrieval latency\n\n\*\*Skip reranking (for now) when:\*\*\n\n- Your corpus is small and tightly scoped (under ~500 chunks, homogeneous content) — embedding similarity is probably precise enough\n- Your p95 retrieval latency is already at the edge of your SLA and you have no budget to absorb 100–400ms more\n- Queries are keyword-style lookups rather than natural language questions — BM25 + vector hybrid often handles those cleanly without a reranker\n- You haven't instrumented your pipeline yet — you need eval data before you can measure whether reranking is actually the fix\n\nThat last point matters. \*\*Don't add reranking blind.\*\* Build a small eval set first: 50–100 (query, expected answer, source chunk) triples. Measure retrieval precision before and after. If your recall@5 jumps from 0.6 to 0.85, reranking is working. If it moves less than 5 points, look elsewhere.\n\n---\n\n# Before/after: what the pipeline looks like\n\n\*\*Before (standard RAG):\*\*\n\n- User query → embed → vector search → top-10 chunks by cosine similarity → prompt with 10 chunks → LLM\n\n\*\*After (RAG + rerank):\*\*\n\n- User query → embed → vector search → top-30 candidate chunks → reranker scores each (query, chunk) pair → top-5 by relevance score → prompt with 5 chunks → LLM\n\nThe prompt is shorter. The chunks are better matched. Token cost per query often drops 30–50% even though you're running a reranker — because you pulled back from top-10 to top-5, and the 5 you kept are the right ones.\n\nThe engineering change is roughly:\n\n- Add a reranker call between retrieval and prompt assembly\n- Adjust your top-k logic to pull more candidates from the index (recall stage) and fewer into the prompt (precision stage)\n- Add relevance score logging so you can monitor reranker output over time\n\nThat's a day of work for a senior engineer, possibly two if you're setting up self-hosted infrastructure. The Cohere API path is closer to half a day.\n\n---\n\n# The observability piece you can't skip\n\nReranking improves things, but you need to measure it or you're flying blind. The signals worth tracking:\n\n- \*\*Reranker score distribution\*\* — if scores are clustered near 0 for the top result, your retrieval candidate set is bad and reranking can't save it\n- \*\*Top-1 chunk relevance\*\* — spot-check whether the highest-scored chunk actually answers the query. Do this manually for 20–30 queries after shipping\n- \*\*Answer quality on your eval set\*\* — run your evals before and after. Measure retrieval precision@k and, if you have ground truth, answer correctness\n- \*\*Latency p50 / p95\*\* — reranking adds latency; know your baseline before so you can measure the delta honestly\n\nIf you don't have an eval set, that's the thing to build first. A RAG pipeline without evals is a system you can't improve systematically — you're just guessing.\n\n---\n\n# The broader principle\n\nThe pattern here applies beyond just reranking. Most RAG quality problems have a two-stage fix:\n\n- Measure what's actually breaking (retrieval precision? context order? LLM instruction-following?)\n- Apply the smallest change that moves the metric you measured\n\nReranking is the highest-ROI intervention for retrieval precision in most production RAG stacks. It's not the only one, and it's not a substitute for good chunking or a clean index. But if you've already shipped a RAG feature and you're chasing quality complaints, it's almost always the first place to look.\n\nWe've added reranking to pipelines across support bots, internal knowledge tools, and document Q\u0026A systems. The pattern holds: modest latency cost, measurable quality improvement, no change to your existing index or embedding model.\n\n---\n\n\*\*Next step:\*\* Pull your last 50 failed or low-quality queries from your RAG feature's logs. Score each one manually: did the right chunk appear in the top-5 results? If fewer than 70% did, reranking will help you. If you want a second pair of eyes on your retrieval architecture before you make changes, our team offers a fixed-fee technical review — book a call at alian.ai and tell us what you're working with."])self.\_\_next\_f.push([1,"12:T29dc,"])self.\_\_next\_f.push([1,"# The problem is usually the contract, not the vendor\n\nYou've shortlisted two or three AI delivery partners. Their tech stacks look similar. Their portfolios are credible. Then the proposals land and none of them match each other — one quotes a fixed-fee sprint, one wants a six-month retainer, one sends an hourly rate sheet and asks you to estimate hours. Now you're comparing apples to aircraft carriers, and the decision you actually need to make — \*which contract structure protects me?\* — has been swallowed by a negotiation you weren't ready for.\n\nThis post is not about which vendor to hire. It's about the contract structure decision that comes before that. Get this wrong and a strong team will still deliver a weak outcome: scope balloons, cash exposure grows, and the project stalls waiting for the next invoice cycle instead of shipping.\n\n# Three models, three risk profiles\n\nBefore comparing them, it's worth being precise about what each model actually is.\n\n\*\*Fixed-fee Sprint\*\* — a scoped, time-boxed engagement with a single price agreed upfront. Typically 2–8 weeks. You know the total cost before day one.\n\n\*\*Monthly Retainer\*\* — a recurring monthly fee that buys a defined number of hours or a defined team slice. Usually requires a minimum commitment (3 months is common). Cost is predictable per month, but total cost accumulates with time.\n\n\*\*Hourly\*\* — you pay for actual hours logged, usually against a monthly minimum. Total cost is variable. You have maximum flexibility to redirect effort, but maximum exposure to scope creep.\n\nEach one shifts the risk differently. The wrong choice doesn't mean you hired a bad vendor — it means the incentive structure is misaligned with what your project actually needs at this stage.\n\n# When a fixed-fee sprint is the right call\n\nA sprint works when \*\*what needs to be built is knowable in advance\*\* — or when the explicit goal is to \*make\* it knowable.\n\nUse a sprint for:\n\n- A proof-of-concept agent you want in production within 4–6 weeks to test with real users\n- A first milestone on a larger roadmap where you want to de-risk before committing further budget\n- A bounded integration — e.g., connecting an LLM to your existing support queue and shipping an auto-draft feature\n- Any situation where you're not yet sure you want a long-term relationship with this vendor\n\nSprint pricing typically lands in the \*\*$15,000–$60,000 range\*\* depending on complexity and seniority of the build. You know that number on day one. Your cash exposure is capped.\n\nThe tradeoff: fixed-fee sprints require a well-defined scope. If your requirements are genuinely uncertain — you're still figuring out the problem, not the solution — a sprint either inflates the price to cover the vendor's risk, or it produces something that doesn't fit what you discover mid-build. Both outcomes are bad.\n\n\*\*A sprint is not a retainer with a deadline.\*\* It's a specific deliverable at a specific price. If a vendor quotes you a sprint but the scope document is vague, that's a warning sign: you're about to pay a fixed fee for an undefined outcome.\n\n# When a monthly retainer makes sense\n\nA retainer works when \*\*the work is continuous, the relationship needs to compound, and you need flexibility in what gets built week to week\*\*.\n\nUse a retainer for:\n\n- Ongoing agent maintenance, evals, and prompt iteration after an initial build is live\n- A team augmentation arrangement where you're adding senior AI engineering capacity to an existing internal team\n- A product roadmap with multiple features shipping across a quarter, where priorities shift based on user feedback\n- Long-term observability, cost monitoring, and model upgrade cycles\n\nRetainer pricing depends heavily on the team slice. A single senior AI architect running 20 hours a month is a very different cost profile than a two-person team running 80 hours a month. At $35/hr for senior architects and $25/hr for engineering, a 40-hour monthly retainer runs roughly \*\*$1,000–$1,400/month at minimum\*\*; a substantive team engagement is more likely \*\*$8,000–$20,000/month\*\* depending on seniority mix and hours committed.\n\nThe risk with a retainer is drift. Without clear monthly deliverables written into the agreement, retainers can become expensive status-quo arrangements where hours get logged but outcomes don't compound. Before signing a retainer, ask: \*what does success look like at month three, and how will we know we've hit it?\* If the vendor can't answer that, the retainer is protecting them, not you.\n\n\*\*Retainers also require a minimum commitment to be worthwhile\*\* — typically three months. That's meaningful cash exposure if the relationship goes sideways in month one. Which is exactly why a sprint-first structure makes sense: ship something real in a fixed-fee engagement, validate the working relationship, then move to a retainer for the sustained phase.\n\n# When hourly is actually the right fit\n\nHourly gets a bad reputation, but it's the correct structure for specific situations.\n\nUse hourly for:\n\n- \*\*Audit and advisory work\*\* — you want a senior architect to review your current AI stack, identify failure modes, and produce a written assessment. You know roughly how many hours this takes and you don't need a long-term structure around it.\n- \*\*Unblocking a specific technical problem\*\* — you have an internal team that's stuck on something. You want expert hours against that problem, not a full engagement.\n- \*\*Pre-sprint discovery\*\* — before scoping a fixed-fee sprint, you want 10–20 hours of technical discovery to make sure the sprint scope is actually accurate.\n\nThe risk with hourly is obvious: without a ceiling, costs are unbounded. If you're using hourly for anything other than a bounded, advisory-style engagement, build in a monthly hour cap and a written checkpoint — \"at 15 hours, we review scope before proceeding.\"\n\nAt a 20-hour monthly minimum, hourly works out to \*\*$500–$700/month at entry level\*\* — but that's rarely what people actually use it for. The situations where hourly makes sense tend to involve senior architects, which means the relevant range is \*\*$700–$1,400 for a 20-hour block at $35/hr\*\*.\n\n# The cash exposure comparison, week by week\n\nThis is where the choice gets concrete. Imagine you're three weeks into an engagement that's gone sideways. What's your exposure?\n\n- \*\*Fixed-fee sprint, 6-week engagement at $30,000:\*\* You've likely paid 50% upfront ($15,000) and owe 50% on delivery. If the project stalls, your exposure is the upfront payment. You have leverage to demand the deliverable before releasing the final payment.\n- \*\*Monthly retainer at $12,000/month with a 3-month minimum:\*\* Three weeks in, you've spent roughly $9,000 and are contractually committed to $36,000 total. If something's wrong, you're negotiating out of a signed commitment, not just withholding a milestone payment.\n- \*\*Hourly at $35/hr, no cap, 3 weeks of heavy work:\*\* If the vendor logged 80 hours across three weeks — not unusual for an active build — you're at $2,800, with no contracted limit on what comes next unless you wrote one in.\n\nNone of these is the \"safe\" option in absolute terms. \*\*The safe option is the one that matches the structure of your risk.\*\* If you're certain about scope, the sprint protects you. If you're uncertain about scope but certain about the ongoing need, the retainer protects you — provided you negotiate monthly deliverables and an exit clause.\n\n# The vendor lock-in question\n\nContract structure and lock-in are related but separate. You can sign a well-structured retainer and still end up locked in if the IP, code, and prompts stay with the vendor.\n\nBefore signing anything, get clear answers to:\n\n- Who owns the code on day one — not on project completion, but immediately as it's written?\n- Who owns the prompts, the fine-tuned model weights, the eval harnesses?\n- If we end the engagement at month two of a three-month retainer, what do we walk away with?\n\nAt Alian AI, code and IP transfer to the client on day one — not at the end of the contract. That's a structural choice, not a marketing claim, and it matters most when engagements end early or pivot mid-flight. Any vendor that hedges on this question — or ties IP transfer to payment completion on a long retainer — is building lock-in into the contract structure itself.\n\n# The sprint-then-retainer pattern\n\nThe most common mistake we see is founders trying to decide between a sprint and a retainer as if they're mutually exclusive. They're not. The most de-risked path for a new AI engagement usually looks like this:\n\n- \*\*Weeks 1–6:\*\* Fixed-fee sprint to ship a defined first milestone — a working agent, a RAG pipeline, an integration. You validate the vendor, the architecture, and the actual shape of the problem before committing further.\n- \*\*Months 2–6+:\*\* Retainer for the sustained phase — ongoing evals, feature iteration, cost monitoring, model upgrades. Now you're paying for continuity on a relationship you've already validated.\n\nThis pattern keeps early cash exposure low, gives you a natural off-ramp if the sprint deliverable disappoints, and sets up a retainer on terms you negotiate from a position of knowing exactly what you got in the first phase.\n\n# What to do before you sign\n\n- \*\*Map your certainty level.\*\* Is the scope of this work clear enough to fix-fee, or is discovery still ongoing? If ongoing, don't sign a sprint with a vague scope document.\n- \*\*Ask for a sprint-first option\*\* even if the vendor's default proposal is a retainer. A vendor confident in their work will agree to demonstrate it in a bounded engagement before locking in a longer commitment.\n- \*\*Negotiate monthly deliverables into any retainer\*\* — not just hours logged. \"20 hours/month\" is a vendor commitment. \"Shipped evals framework and updated prompt library by end of month one\" is a client commitment.\n- \*\*Clarify IP transfer in writing\*\* before the contract is signed, not in a follow-up email after.\n- \*\*Build in a review gate at month one\*\* on any retainer longer than two months. The engagement should have to earn its continuation.\n\nIf you're currently looking at proposals and trying to make sense of the structure differences, the [Alian AI engagement models page](/services) lays out exactly how our sprints, retainers, and hourly blocks are structured — including what's included in each and what the minimum commitments are. Or if you want to talk through which model fits your specific stage and scope, book a 30-minute call with our team and we'll tell you honestly which structure we'd recommend — even if it's a shorter engagement than what we'd prefer to sell."])self.\_\_next\_f.push([1,"13:Tb7b,"])self.\_\_next\_f.push([1,"# The eval-at-launch trap\n\nMost teams treat evals like a launch checklist. Write 20 test cases, run them, ship the model, move on. Six months later the model is silently degraded, the team doesn't know, and a customer escalation surfaces what should have been caught weeks earlier.\n\nThe right mental model: evals are a permanent system. Closer to your monitoring stack than to your test suite.\n\n# What permanent means in practice\n\nEval suites grow weekly. Every production failure we catch becomes a permanent case in the suite. The suite runs against every prompt change, every model change, and weekly against production output regardless.\n\nFailing the eval blocks merge. Drift over time triggers a model review. The owner of the suite (yes, named owner) reports to the same review cadence as the rest of the engineering org.\n\n# The 20-case minimum, and the 200-case ceiling\n\n20 hand-picked cases beat 200 synthetic ones. The 20 cover happy path, common edge cases, refusal patterns, and the patterns you can't afford to break. Below 20 you're not really testing. Above ~200 you're spending more cycles on eval maintenance than on the agent itself.\n\nThe healthy distribution we see in client projects: 60% real anonymized production cases, 30% adversarial / edge cases we wrote, 10% regression cases pulled from past bugs.\n\n# LLM-as-judge — where it works, where it breaks\n\nLLM-as-judge is the only way to score evals at scale. For most criteria (faithfulness, refusal correctness, tone match) it works well enough.\n\nIt breaks on three things:\n\n1. \*\*Subtle factual accuracy.\*\* When the ground truth requires domain expertise the judge model doesn't have, scores get noisy.\n2. \*\*Subjective quality.\*\* \"Is this tactful enough?\" — humans disagree, and the judge model often agrees with whichever side phrased the prompt.\n3. \*\*Multi-turn coherence.\*\* Judge models score individual turns better than whole conversations.\n\nFix: human review on the close calls. Score 80%+ of cases automatically with LLM-as-judge, route the bottom decile to a human. That's where tuning judgment lives.\n\n# Cost telemetry inside the eval suite\n\nEvery eval run logs token cost. You learn how much each test case costs and which ones are pulling the average up. Over time you spot \"this prompt change improved quality but tripled cost\" before the bill arrives.\n\nWe build this into every eval suite we ship now. It started as a defensive measure and turned into a useful product-economics signal.\n\n# Who owns it after we leave\n\nThis is the question we ask in every handoff. If the answer is \"we'll figure it out\", we know the eval suite will atrophy. If there's a named human with the eval review on their calendar, it usually survives.\n\nWe've started writing the named owner into the SOW. Not as a deliverable from us — as a commitment from the client. The clients who push back are the ones whose evals will die. We bring this up before signing, not after."])self.\_\_next\_f.push([1,"14:Taa2,"])self.\_\_next\_f.push([1,"# Why e-commerce is the easiest AI win in 2026\n\nE-commerce has the cleanest AI ROI math of any vertical. Tickets per week × handle time × loaded cost gives you a number. Deflect 40-60% with an agent that reads your docs. Subtract build + ops cost. The decision makes itself.\n\nWe've shipped variations of these in the last 18 months. Here's the order we'd run them in if we were greenfielding.\n\n## Phase 1 (weeks 1–6): Support deflection bot\n\nStart here. Highest, fastest, most measurable ROI. RAG over your docs, policy pages, FAQ, and the top 100 historical tickets. Citation-required. Refusal patterns. Escalation queue.\n\nMost B2C clients deflect 50-70% of WISMO, returns, and policy questions in the first 90 days. The dollar value of that on a 500-tickets-per-week store usually pays for the build in under 4 months.\n\n## Phase 2 (weeks 4–10, parallel): Catalog assistant\n\nPDP-embedded assistant that answers fit, size, compatibility, and \"which one should I get\" with citations from product copy and reviews. Lifts conversion 8-20% on PDP traffic in our deployments — and feels like a real assistant, not a chatbot.\n\nWe pair this with the Phase 1 bot in the same chat surface — the user doesn't know which agent is answering, just that the answer is good.\n\n## Phase 3 (weeks 8–14): Review intelligence\n\nAggregate reviews across platforms (Trustpilot, Google, Amazon, your own). Extract themes, sentiment, surface PDP-ready quotes. Update PDPs weekly with the highest-converting quote.\n\nThe under-appreciated win: theme extraction surfaces product issues you wouldn't have found in your support tickets.\n\n## Phase 4 (ongoing): Product copy at scale\n\nBrand-voice fine-tuned generator that produces PDP copy from spec sheets. Editor-in-the-loop. Output gets faster and more consistent over time.\n\nNot as high-ROI as the support bot, but compounds — every new SKU is faster than the last.\n\n## What to skip in 2026\n\n- \*\*AI image generation for products.\*\* Customers want real product photos. Use AI for thumbnails and social variants, not catalog photography.\n- \*\*Personalization-only chatbots.\*\* \"Hi {first\_name}\" is not the point. Substantive product help is.\n- \*\*Email content generation in isolation.\*\* Generated emails without measurement loops underperform. Ship them with eval loops or skip them.\n\n## What this typically costs\n\nPhases 1+2 together run $50-90K loaded over 6-10 weeks. Phase 3 adds $20-30K. Phase 4 is a $25-40K initial build then ongoing usage. A complete pass at a mid-market e-com store is $100-160K — and almost always pays back in under 9 months on a $5M+ revenue store.\n\nIf you'd rather phase the spend, start with support deflection only. It's the highest-confidence single bet."])self.\_\_next\_f.push([1,"15:Tfcd,"])self.\_\_next\_f.push([1,"# The AI hiring market is broken\n\nEveryone with a Coursera certificate is calling themselves an AI engineer. Everyone with three blog posts about RAG is a senior. Pay bands have inflated 30-50% in 18 months. And most of the candidates can't ship anything past a Jupyter notebook to production.\n\nHere's what to actually screen for if you're hiring AI engineers in 2026.\n\n# Resume signals that don't matter\n\n- \*\*Number of courses completed.\*\* Anyone can finish a course. The market is flooded.\n- \*\*Big-co AI team affiliation.\*\* Some of the worst AI engineers we've interviewed came from FAANG AI orgs — they shipped one feature into a stack of 50 and called themselves senior.\n- \*\*GitHub stars on a notebook repo.\*\* Notebooks aren't production. Stars are vanity.\n- \*\*A Hugging Face Space they made.\*\* Same as above. Toy.\n- \*\*Number of LLM-related blog posts.\*\* Sometimes correlates with thinking. Often correlates with marketing.\n\n# Resume signals that do matter\n\n- \*\*Production traffic numbers.\*\* \"I shipped a feature handling X requests/day with Y latency target\" — they have specifics.\n- \*\*Eval suite mentions.\*\* Anyone who's shipped production AI has fought an eval suite into existence. They'll bring it up unprompted.\n- \*\*Cost-aware design.\*\* They'll describe how they optimized for token cost as well as quality.\n- \*\*A failure they own.\*\* \"I shipped this and it broke, here's what we did\" — the most reliable senior signal.\n\n# Interview questions that actually work\n\n\*\*1. \"Tell me about an eval suite you built. How did it evolve over the first 90 days?\"\*\*\n\nStrong candidates describe specific cases that got added, specific regressions caught, and specific things they decided not to score. Weak candidates describe \"we wrote tests.\"\n\n\*\*2. \"Walk me through the cost trade-off on a recent prompt change.\"\*\*\n\nSenior AI engineers think about token cost the way senior backend engineers think about query cost. They have a number per conversation, an opinion about where it's going, and a plan for when it crosses a threshold.\n\n\*\*3. \"Show me a refusal pattern you wrote.\"\*\*\n\nWhen the agent shouldn't answer. Strong candidates have a library of these — they're production scars. Weak candidates think refusal is something the model decides on its own.\n\n\*\*4. \"Describe an agent action you decided not to ship to auto-execute.\"\*\*\n\nThe strong signal: a real example of a decision deferred to a human, with the reasoning. Senior engineers earn auto-execute trust — they don't start with it.\n\n\*\*5. \"What's your default first-call architecture for a customer-support chatbot?\"\*\*\n\nA passable answer covers RAG with hybrid search + reranking, citation-required prompting, refusal patterns, eval suite seeded at 20 cases, escalation queue. A great answer mentions the gotchas (chunk boundaries, citation hallucination, multi-turn state).\n\n# What we screen out\n\n- Candidates who can't reproduce their own results without a notebook.\n- Anyone who's never owned a model in production for 6+ months.\n- Engineers who haven't argued with a PM about a refusal pattern.\n- Anyone who describes LLM development as \"configuring prompts.\"\n\n# The seniority signal that compounds\n\nSenior AI engineers think in production from sentence one. They mention cost, latency, observability, eval suites, refusal patterns, and audit trails without being asked. They've shipped, broken things, fixed them, and have scars.\n\nIf your candidate doesn't talk like that, they're not senior, regardless of their title.\n\n# Compensation reality\n\nUS market for senior AI engineers is $250-400K base + equity at well-funded startups. Bay Area FAANG-equivalents go higher. For most companies, that's tough to compete with.\n\nTwo options that work: pay above-market for a small in-house team (2-3 people, $300K+ each), or partner with a delivery agency where senior engineering hours are $25-150/hour depending on geography. Either is fine. Hybrid is often the best answer.\n\nTrying to hire 8 cheap \"AI engineers\" in 6 months is the worst answer. You'll get 8 people who can't ship."])self.\_\_next\_f.push([1,"16:Tccc,"])self.\_\_next\_f.push([1,"# Manufacturing is where AI gets real\n\nManufacturing AI is unforgiving. You're shipping into physical processes with human safety implications, contractual SLAs, and ERPs that have been working fine for years. The wins are huge — our Multi-Agent Manufacturing case study delivered 31% downtime reduction in 90 days — but only if you pick the right v1.\n\nThese six questions, answered honestly, point at which v1 fits your plant.\n\n## 1. Is your data accessible without IT tickets?\n\nSCADA, MES, ERP, operator logs — each speaks its own dialect. If pulling production data for analysis requires a Friday-afternoon IT ticket, that's your bottleneck. Fix data accessibility before the agent.\n\nIf yes: predictive maintenance and multi-agent ops are on the table. If no: start with document automation (POs, QC reports, invoices), which doesn't need real-time data plumbing.\n\n## 2. Is downtime the #1 measurable cost?\n\nMost plants either know their hourly downtime cost or feel it in their P\u0026L. If you can ballpark it within 20%, predictive maintenance has clean ROI math. If you can't, that's worth fixing before any AI investment.\n\n## 3. Do shifts hand off well?\n\nIf supervisors are spending 20-30 minutes per shift change recapping, a Shift Briefing Agent is a strong v1. Low risk, immediate quality-of-life lift, almost no integration work. Often the entry-point engagement in our multi-agent builds.\n\n## 4. Is your ERP under-used by leadership?\n\nERPs accumulate power that stays locked behind menus. If your CFO can't query cost drift without an analyst, an AI-native ERP layer is high-leverage. We built this for Alian Infinity — 4× leadership-tier usage in the first quarter.\n\n## 5. Do you have a quality engineer who hates running QC?\n\nVision QC is a great wedge. YOLO-based defect detection with active-learning loops. Routes uncertain cases to your QC engineer rather than asking them to look at every part. Most pay back in under 6 months on a high-volume line.\n\n## 6. Is on-prem a hard requirement?\n\nIf yes, plan on edge gateway deployments + cloud reasoning over scoped channels, or fully on-prem with open-source models. Slower iteration, but doable. We've shipped both.\n\nIf no, you can default to multi-region cloud, which iterates much faster.\n\n## What to skip in v1\n\n- Full multi-agent system as your first build. Start with 1-2 agents max. The org chart pattern is right, but build it incrementally.\n- A \"central plant AI\". Specialized agents per role beat one general model every time.\n- Replacing supervisors. AI watches. Humans decide. Always.\n\n## Typical first-build cost\n\nA scoped 2-agent v1 (e.g., shift briefing + maintenance scheduler) on a single plant: $50-80K loaded over 8-10 weeks. Full multi-agent system: $90-150K over 12-16 weeks. Predictive maintenance only: $40-70K over 6-8 weeks.\n\nROI on downtime-reduction builds is usually 6-9 months. Document automation typically pays back in 4-6.\n\n## Where to start the conversation\n\nIf you answered \"yes\" to questions 1+2: predictive maintenance or multi-agent ops.\n\nIf \"yes\" to 3 only: shift briefing agent (easy first build).\n\nIf \"yes\" to 4: AI-native ERP layer.\n\nIf \"yes\" to 5: vision QC.\n\nIf you're not sure: book a 90-minute discovery and we'll walk your plant through the questions ourselves."])self.\_\_next\_f.push([1,"17:Tf2e,"])self.\_\_next\_f.push([1,"# What fintech compliance teams actually need from AI\n\nFintech AI engagements get killed at compliance review more often than at engineering review. Not because the AI is dangerous — but because the compliance team's questions don't have clean answers, and \"we'll figure it out\" doesn't land in a regulated context.\n\nHere's the compliance pattern we ship on every fintech engagement. It's not exotic; it's just non-optional.\n\n## Audit trails on every AI-generated decision\n\nEvery prompt, retrieval, tool call, and output gets logged with reasoning. Tied to a named user, named system, and a unique decision ID. Retained for the lifetime of the underlying record (not just 90 days).\n\nAuditors don't ask \"is the AI right?\" They ask \"can you show me what happened on March 14, account 4729?\" If you can answer that with timestamps and reasoning, you pass. If not, you don't.\n\n## Model approval workflow\n\nBefore any production AI touches a regulated workflow, it gets reviewed and signed off by a named owner. We build the approval workflow into the deployment system — you literally can't deploy without a recorded approval.\n\nIncludes: model card, prompt version, eval scores, intended scope, refusal patterns, escalation path. Stored together. Versioned.\n\n## Refusal patterns + escalation\n\nThe AI must be able to say \"I don't know — let me route this.\" More important: it must do this on every category your policy doesn't allow it to answer. Not via a model decision — via explicit policy at the orchestration layer.\n\nWe document refusal categories in plain English (the auditor reads them) and in code (the engineer enforces them). Both have to match.\n\n## Eval coverage with regulator-relevant cases\n\nEval suites include cases derived from past audit findings, internal control reviews, and known failure modes. Replayed weekly. Failure rate is a SOX-equivalent metric in our engagements.\n\n## Read your DPA. Then read it again.\n\nAnthropic, OpenAI, and your other vendors have DPAs. They say specific things about data handling, training opt-outs, retention, and sub-processors. Your compliance team needs to read each one. We don't paper-over with \"we use enterprise tiers\" — we surface the actual document and walk through it.\n\n## SOC 2 readiness ≠ SOC 2 audit\n\n\"SOC 2 ready\" means we build to controls the auditor will look for. The audit is between you and your auditor — we're not the auditor, and any vendor claiming \"SOC 2 certified\" without the report is doing word-play.\n\nWe have the controls. The report is yours to commission.\n\n## Data residency\n\nFor most fintech clients we keep inference in the region of record. Anthropic and OpenAI both offer regional inference now. Edge gateway + open-source models when even the regional cloud is too much.\n\nThis is a 30-minute discussion at the start of every engagement. Don't skip it.\n\n## What compliance teams actually ask\n\nReal questions we've gotten from regulated-finance compliance teams, in our experience:\n\n1. \"How do you stop the model from making things up about our policy?\"\n2. \"Show me an example audit trail end-to-end.\"\n3. \"Who approved the prompt? What did they approve specifically?\"\n4. \"How do you know the model is still doing what it was approved to do?\"\n5. \"What happens when the AI is wrong?\"\n\nIf your AI build doesn't have clean, unambiguous answers to all five, it's not production-ready in a fintech context — regardless of how good the model is.\n\n## What this adds to the build cost\n\nCompliance-grade AI adds 20-40% to a standard build, mostly in the audit-trail plumbing, the approval workflow, and the eval coverage. We don't strip those costs even on small engagements — the cost of building them in after the fact is much higher.\n\nIf your compliance team is upstream of your engineering team in priority, that's the right order. Build for the audit. The engineering work falls out of it."])self.\_\_next\_f.push([1,"18:Tf2c,"])self.\_\_next\_f.push([1,"# The build, decoded\n\nWe've published a case study on the Multi-Agent Manufacturing System we shipped — 31% downtime reduction, 90 days, etc. The numbers there are real. This post unpacks what's underneath them: cost, complexity, what worked first try, what didn't.\n\n# What it actually does\n\nSix specialized agents — Production Monitor, Maintenance Scheduler, Quality Anomaly, Inventory/Reorder, Energy Optimization, Shift Briefing — running 24/7 across a 40-machine sheet metal plant. They communicate through a shared event bus (Postgres). Every alert routes to a human via WhatsApp and the existing supervisor dashboard.\n\nNo agent takes physical action on its own. Humans always pull the trigger.\n\n# Build cost\n\nFixed-fee Sprint: 12 weeks. Total build cost was in the range of $90K–$120K loaded. Engagement-specific factors moved the number:\n\n- \*\*Data integration work.\*\* SCADA, ERP, operator tablets — three systems with three different access patterns. The first month was almost entirely data plumbing.\n- \*\*On-prem edge gateway.\*\* Latency and data-residency requirements meant we deployed an edge gateway. Hardware was minor (NUC-class), but the deployment work added 2 weeks.\n- \*\*Six agents instead of one.\*\* Each agent is a separate LangGraph workflow with its own tools, eval suite, and observability hooks. The marginal cost of agents 2–6 was much lower than agent 1, but it's still real.\n\n# Run cost\n\nMonthly operating cost (after launch) is roughly $1,800/month:\n\n- Anthropic API spend: $1,200/month (varies with shift volume)\n- Edge gateway compute + cloud reasoning: $400/month\n- Observability + logging: $200/month\n\nA Monthly Retainer ($X — call it 60 hours/month) layered on top, used for: weekly model retraining, eval coverage as the plant added new machines, two new agent additions in the second quarter.\n\n# What worked first try\n\n- \*\*One agent per role.\*\* Mirroring the org chart turned out to be the right mental model. Operators understood it immediately.\n- \*\*WhatsApp routing.\*\* Operators don't read email and don't sit at a dashboard. WhatsApp was the only channel that mattered.\n- \*\*Postgres as the event bus.\*\* Kafka was overkill. Postgres was already in the stack. Skipping Kafka saved 2 weeks.\n\n# What we'd do differently\n\n- \*\*Heavier discovery on shift handoffs.\*\* The Shift Briefing Agent was the dark-horse win, but we underspeced it. Two weeks more discovery would've gotten the format right faster.\n- \*\*Tighter eval on the Maintenance Scheduler.\*\* This agent makes calendar decisions; the eval signal was lagging. We bolted on a synthetic test harness in week 8 — should've been week 1.\n- \*\*Skipped LightGBM in v1.\*\* We layered classical ML for downtime prediction. In hindsight, simple thresholding would've covered 80% of value in v1, and ML could've come in v2 once the agent was trusted.\n\n# What to ask before you green-light a build like this\n\n1. \*\*Is your data accessible?\*\* SCADA + ERP + operator inputs all needed plumbing. If yours is locked behind vendors, double the timeline.\n2. \*\*Do you have a human-in-loop owner?\*\* The supervisor on shift is the one who acts on the agent's signals. If that ownership isn't clear, build a dashboard first, an agent later.\n3. \*\*Are you ready to run it?\*\* Monthly retraining and eval coverage is a 5–15 hour/month commitment. If no one owns that, the model drifts and you blame the agent.\n4. \*\*Is the downtime cost honest?\*\* Our client knew their hourly downtime cost to the penny. That made the ROI math obvious. If you can't ballpark yours within 20%, do that first.\n\n# What it'd cost for you\n\nA scoped version of this — say 2–3 agents instead of 6, no on-prem requirement, cleaner data — runs $50K–$75K loaded over 8–10 weeks. With on-prem, multi-site, or a heavier ML component, plan on $90K–$150K.\n\nIf the math doesn't work, it doesn't work. We'll tell you on the call."])self.\_\_next\_f.push([1,"19:Ta70,"])self.\_\_next\_f.push([1,"# 90 days is the right horizon\n\nSix months is too long — strategy decays. Thirty days is too short — nothing real ships. Ninety days lets you discover, pilot, and ship one production use case while leadership keeps their attention on it.\n\n# The plan\n\n## Weeks 1–2: Discovery + audit\n\n- Interview 8–12 people across product, eng, ops, finance, and support. Cheap insurance against building the wrong thing.\n- Audit data accessibility: what lives where, what's stale, what's siloed.\n- Score 30 use cases against impact / feasibility / data readiness.\n- End of week 2: a ranked backlog and one pilot candidate that's ready to scope.\n\n## Weeks 3–4: Pilot scope + SOW\n\n- Tight scope for the pilot — one workflow, one team, one measurable outcome.\n- Define success metrics upfront. \"We'll know it worked if X moves Y in 60 days.\"\n- Build-vs-buy on the model layer. Default: hosted API. Don't reach for fine-tuning yet.\n- Sign-off from the pilot owner and budget owner before any code lands.\n\n## Weeks 5–10: Build\n\n- Working code in week 5. If you don't have something to demo by end of week 5, the scope was wrong.\n- Weekly demos to the pilot team. Not the leadership team — the pilot team.\n- Eval suite from day one. Twenty test cases per use case, run weekly.\n- Bi-weekly retros. Catch direction changes early when they're cheap.\n\n## Weeks 11–12: Ship + measure\n\n- Production deploy to the pilot team. Real users, real tickets, real data.\n- Daily readout for the first week. Catch regressions in hours, not days.\n- Measure against the success metrics from week 4. Honest read-out at week 12.\n\n## Week 12+: Decide\n\n- The pilot succeeded? Pick the next two use cases and start over.\n- The pilot didn't? Honest postmortem. Was it the model, the data, the workflow, or the framing?\n\n# What to NOT do in the first 90 days\n\n- Don't build a platform. Build a use case. Platforms are what you wish you'd built after three use cases — not before.\n- Don't fine-tune anything. Prompts + RAG covers 85% of the value with a 10x faster iteration loop.\n- Don't try to centralize all AI procurement in week 1. You'll spend the 90 days writing a policy doc and shipping nothing.\n- Don't try to govern what you haven't built yet. Governance comes after you've seen what production AI actually does.\n\n# What this costs\n\nFor a mid-market company, plan on $40K–$120K for the pilot (depends on use case complexity). The strategy phase (weeks 1–4) can be $15K–$30K standalone if you want to scope before you commit to the build.\n\nIf you don't have the budget for both, do the strategy phase only. A 30-page report and a ranked backlog is worth more than a half-built agent."])self.\_\_next\_f.push([1,"1a:T999,"])self.\_\_next\_f.push([1,"# Why support agents are the SMB's wedge into AI\n\nCustomer-support volume scales linearly with revenue, but the answers don't. 70% of tier-1 tickets are repeat questions your docs already answer — but no user reads docs. An AI agent that reads the docs for them deflects that 70%, and the back half (the ones humans should actually touch) gets routed cleaner.\n\n# The architecture we ship\n\nA production support agent has six moving parts. Most \"AI chatbot\" attempts skip three of them and wonder why the thing hallucinates.\n\n1. \*\*Intent classifier\*\* — routes the ticket into resolvable / needs-human / docs-gap / feature-request.\n2. \*\*Retrieval\*\* — hybrid search (BM25 + vector) over your docs, with reranking. Citations required.\n3. \*\*Answer generation\*\* — Claude or GPT, prompted to refuse when retrieval confidence is low.\n4. \*\*Escalation path\*\* — a confidence threshold and a human queue. Non-negotiable.\n5. \*\*Eval loop\*\* — 200 historical tickets replayed weekly. Drift kills support bots quietly.\n6. \*\*Cost telemetry\*\* — every conversation logged with token cost, deflection outcome, CSAT.\n\nSkip the eval loop and you'll ship a great demo that's silently degraded in 90 days.\n\n# The hard parts\n\nThe model is not the hard part. The hard parts are:\n\n- \*\*Knowing when not to answer.\*\* A good support agent says \"I'm not sure — let me route this to a human\" more often than it answers.\n- \*\*Multi-turn state.\*\* \"I tried that, didn't work\" needs to remember what 'that' was.\n- \*\*Tool use.\*\* The agent often needs to look up the user's account, recent activity, or feature flags before it can answer. That means scoped read access to your systems.\n\n# What this costs to build\n\nFor a B2B SaaS with a few hundred tickets/week and a moderate docs base, a Fixed-fee Sprint runs 5–8 weeks and includes the eval loop and the first three months of tuning. After that, most clients move to a Monthly Retainer for ongoing eval coverage as the product changes.\n\nIf your docs are a mess, add 2 weeks for a docs cleanup pass — without it, the agent's only as good as what it can retrieve.\n\n# Start with deflection math\n\nBefore you build, do the math. Tickets per week × avg handle time × loaded support cost = the dollar volume of tier-1 you're paying for. A working support agent deflects 30–60% of that. If the deflection dollars beat the build + ongoing ops cost in under 9 months, build it. If not, the docs cleanup might be the better project."])self.\_\_next\_f.push([1,"1b:T93d,"])self.\_\_next\_f.push([1,"# The default answer is RAG\n\nIf you're an e-commerce business asking \"should we fine-tune a model on our catalog?\", the answer is almost always: not yet. Start with retrieval-augmented generation (RAG). It's cheaper, faster to iterate, and handles the most common cases — product Q\u0026A, sizing, fit, recommendations grounded in actual inventory.\n\n# When to use RAG (most cases)\n\n- \*\*Product catalog Q\u0026A\*\* — \"does this jacket come in tall sizes?\"\n- \*\*Policy and shipping\*\* — \"what's your return window for sale items in EU?\"\n- \*\*Recommendations grounded in inventory\*\* — \"what would go with this dress that's in stock in size 8?\"\n- \*\*Comparisons\*\* — \"what's the difference between these two SKUs?\"\n\nRAG works because the answers live in structured data (inventory, policy docs, product descriptions). You don't need the model to learn anything new — you need it to retrieve, ground, and explain.\n\n# When to fine-tune\n\n- \*\*Brand voice on long-form content\*\* — product descriptions, editorial, post-purchase emails. Fine-tuning teaches the model to write like you, which prompting can't fully replicate at scale.\n- \*\*Visual classification\*\* — defective vs OK photos, style tags, color naming. Vision fine-tuning beats prompting decisively on these tasks.\n- \*\*Speed/cost at scale\*\* — once you've nailed prompt+RAG, fine-tuning a smaller model on your distilled outputs cuts inference cost 5–10x.\n\n# When to do both\n\nThe mature setup is \*\*fine-tune + retrieval\*\*. You fine-tune for voice and structure; you retrieve for current data. Catalog changes every day — that's RAG. Brand voice changes never — that's fine-tuning.\n\n# The flowchart\n\n1. Is the data you need static or changing daily? → static = fine-tune candidate, dynamic = RAG.\n2. Is the task generation in your voice, or classification / fact retrieval? → voice = fine-tune, retrieval = RAG.\n3. Are you paying $$$/month in inference at scale? → distill a fine-tune later, after RAG works.\n\n# Build sequence we recommend\n\nPhase 1 (4–6 weeks): RAG over catalog + policy. Citation-required. Ship.\nPhase 2 (2–4 weeks): If you're seeing voice drift on long-form, fine-tune a small model on your best-performing product copy.\nPhase 3 (ongoing): As inference cost matters, distill a smaller model from your prompt+RAG outputs.\n\nMost e-commerce clients never reach Phase 3. That's fine."])self.\_\_next\_f.push([1,"1c:T1042,"])self.\_\_next\_f.push([1,"# Why n8n + GPT is the right starting point\n\nZapier is great for IFTTT-style glue. n8n is what you reach for when the glue needs to think — branch on extracted entities, summarize an email thread, classify intent before routing. Pair self-hosted n8n with an LLM API and you can replace 5–10 hours/week of senior ops time per workflow.\n\nWe've shipped variations of these seven for SMB and mid-market clients. Each one pays for itself in under 6 months.\n\n# 1. Invoice triage and entry\n\n\*\*The job:\*\* AP receives 100+ invoices/week as PDFs in a shared inbox. Someone keys them into the accounting system. Errors cost reconciliation hours.\n\n\*\*The workflow:\*\* n8n watches the inbox → GPT extracts vendor, line items, totals, dates → validates against open POs → flags exceptions to a human → posts clean ones to the accounting API.\n\n\*\*Result:\*\* AP team reclaimed ~12 hours/week. Error rate dropped from 4% to 0.6%.\n\n# 2. Inbound lead enrichment\n\n\*\*The job:\*\* Marketing pulls in leads from forms, downloads, webinars. SDRs spend 20 mins per lead enriching: company size, industry, recent funding, tech stack.\n\n\*\*The workflow:\*\* New lead → n8n triggers → calls enrichment APIs (Apollo, Clearbit, BuiltWith) → GPT writes a 3-sentence briefing → pushes everything to HubSpot → notifies the assigned SDR.\n\n\*\*Result:\*\* SDR enrichment time → 0. Time-to-first-touch dropped from 4h to 12min.\n\n# 3. Support ticket pre-classification\n\n\*\*The job:\*\* Support inbox is a mix of bugs, billing, feature requests, partnership inquiries, and noise. Someone triages.\n\n\*\*The workflow:\*\* Inbound email → GPT classifier → routes to the right Linear/Jira project with severity guess → drafts a first-response that the human can edit and send.\n\n\*\*Result:\*\* Triage time 90% down. Average response time on bugs dropped from 6h to 35min.\n\n# 4. Weekly competitor digest\n\n\*\*The job:\*\* Product and marketing want to track 12 competitors — pricing changes, feature launches, blog posts, hiring signals.\n\n\*\*The workflow:\*\* Cron-triggered weekly → n8n scrapes competitor sites, RSS, LinkedIn job posts → GPT summarizes diff vs last week → posts a one-page digest to Slack.\n\n\*\*Result:\*\* Replaces a half-day analyst job. Catches pricing changes within 48h.\n\n# 5. Content distribution pipeline\n\n\*\*The job:\*\* Long-form blog post → repackage for LinkedIn, Twitter/X, email newsletter, internal Slack share. Marketing handcrafts each.\n\n\*\*The workflow:\*\* New blog publish → n8n triggers → GPT generates a LinkedIn version, a 4-tweet thread, an email teaser, and a Slack TL;DR → each goes through one human review → publishes.\n\n\*\*Result:\*\* ~3 hours per post → 25 minutes (mostly review). Doubled distribution surface.\n\n# 6. Renewal risk monitor\n\n\*\*The job:\*\* CSMs need to spot at-risk accounts before the renewal call.\n\n\*\*The workflow:\*\* Weekly cron → pulls usage, support, NPS, and login data → GPT writes a \"risk narrative\" per account with a 1–10 score and the top 3 reasons → CSM dashboard surfaces the bottom decile.\n\n\*\*Result:\*\* Caught 4 churns in the first quarter that the CSM team admitted they'd have missed.\n\n# 7. Compliance evidence collector\n\n\*\*The job:\*\* SOC 2 or ISO 27001 audit needs evidence — access reviews, change tickets, training records — pulled, formatted, and uploaded quarterly.\n\n\*\*The workflow:\*\* Cron-triggered → n8n queries each source system → GPT formats per the auditor's template → uploads to Drive/Vanta → notifies the GRC lead.\n\n\*\*Result:\*\* Saves a week per audit cycle. Auditor satisfaction up because evidence is consistent.\n\n# What these have in common\n\nEvery one of these replaces ops work that's structured but not deterministic — \"look at this, decide what bucket, do the obvious next thing.\" That's the sweet spot for n8n + GPT in 2026. The build cost ranges from $4K (one workflow) to $25K (a coordinated set), and the loaded ops savings typically beat that in 3–6 months.\n\nIf you've got 3+ of these on your wishlist, talk to us about a Monthly Retainer instead of one-off Sprints — the marginal cost of the 4th workflow is much lower when we already have the n8n + GPT scaffolding running."])self.\_\_next\_f.push([1,"5:[[\"$\",\"section\",null,{\"className\":\"px-4 pb-12 pt-16 sm:px-6 lg:px-8\",\"children\":[\"$\",\"div\",null,{\"className\":\"mx-auto max-w-3xl\",\"children\":[[\"$\",\"p\",null,{\"className\":\"text-xs font-semibold uppercase tracking-widest text-[color:var(--color-brand-accent)]\",\"children\":\"Blog\"}],[\"$\",\"h1\",null,{\"className\":\"mt-3 text-3xl font-semibold tracking-tight text-[color:var(--color-text)] sm:text-5xl\",\"children\":\"Things we've shipped — and how.\"}],[\"$\",\"p\",null,{\"className\":\"mt-4 max-w-2xl text-base text-[color:var(--color-muted)] sm:text-lg\",\"children\":\"Practical AI engineering. Architecture, guardrails, eval loops, and the production gotchas no one writes about. # Things we've shipped — and how.

Practical AI engineering. Architecture, guardrails, eval loops, and the production gotchas no one writes about. Filter by tag below.

Filter:All (12)agents (7)automation (1)case-study (1)compliance (1)ecommerce (2)enterprise (1)evals (2)finance (1)fine-tuning (1)governance (1)hiring (1)manufacturing (2)n8n (1)ops (3)production (2)rag (4)roadmap (2)saas (3)strategy (3)team (1)

- [May 28, 2026·8 min read·rag · production

## Reranking Is the Cheapest RAG Improvement You're Not Making

Most teams chase RAG quality by swapping embedding models or tuning chunk sizes. Reranking is a one-day change that routinely does more — here's the architecture, the model choices, and when it's worth the latency cost.

Read →](/blog/reranking-cheapest-rag-improvement)
- [May 28, 2026·9 min read·strategy · ops

## Sprint vs Retainer vs Hourly: How to Pick the Right AI Engagement Model Before You Sign Anything

The wrong contract structure — not the wrong team — is the most common reason AI projects stall or balloon in cost. Here's how to pick the model that actually protects you.

Read →](/blog/sprint-vs-retainer-vs-hourly-ai-engagement-model)
- [May 15, 2026·8 min read·evals · agents

## Evals as a permanent system, not a launch checklist

Why eval suites that ship at launch and then atrophy are the single biggest cause of AI quality decay. The pattern we build instead.

Read →](/blog/evals-as-permanent-system)
- [May 13, 2026·8 min read·ecommerce · agents

## The e-commerce AI playbook for 2026

Catalog assistant, support deflection, product copy at scale, review intelligence — what to ship first, in what order, and what each costs.

Read →](/blog/ecommerce-ai-playbook-2026)
- [May 9, 2026·9 min read·hiring · team

## Hiring for production AI — what to actually screen for

Resume signals that don't matter, interview questions that do, and how to tell a senior AI engineer from someone who can run a notebook.

Read →](/blog/hiring-for-production-ai)
- [May 8, 2026·9 min read·manufacturing · agents

## Manufacturing AI readiness — the 6 questions that decide v1

Multi-agent ops, predictive maintenance, document automation, AI-native ERP. Which one you build first depends on six honest answers about your plant.

Read →](/blog/manufacturing-ai-readiness)
- [May 1, 2026·10 min read·finance · compliance

## AI compliance for fintech: what auditors actually look for

Audit trails, model approval workflows, refusal patterns, and the difference between SOC 2 readiness and SOC 2 audit. The compliance pattern we ship for regulated finance clients.

Read →](/blog/ai-compliance-fintech)
- [Apr 29, 2026·10 min read·manufacturing · agents

## We built an AI agent for manufacturing: here's what it cost and what it does

Real numbers — total build cost, monthly run cost, time-to-value, and what we'd do differently. From our Multi-Agent Manufacturing case study.

Read →](/blog/we-built-ai-agent-manufacturing-cost)
- [Apr 22, 2026·8 min read·strategy · roadmap

Read →](/blog/ai-implementation-roadmap-90-days)

Legal

# Privacy Policy

Last updated: 14 May 2026

[Privacy](/legal/privacy)[Terms](/legal/terms)[Cookies](/legal/cookies)

Alian AI is the AI services division of Alian Software ("we", "us"). This policy explains what data we collect from people who visit `aliansoftware.net` and the Alian AI subdomain, how we use it, and the rights you have.

This is the website privacy policy. Data we process under specific client engagements is governed by the Data Processing Agreement (DPA) signed for that engagement and is not covered here.

## What we collect

**Information you submit.** Name, work email, company, and the problem statement you type into our contact, demo, partner, and AI Readiness Checklist forms. We collect this so we can reply.

**Conversations with our AI agent.** When you use the AI input on the homepage or the floating chat, we send your message to Anthropic for inference and store the input + a summary of the response in our database. We use this to improve responses and to decide which use cases to write about.

**Usage analytics.** If we have analytics enabled (Plausible), we record anonymized pageview and event data without cookies or persistent identifiers.

**Server logs.** Our hosting provider keeps short-term access logs (IP, user agent, requested path) that we use to debug and to enforce rate limits.

## What we don't collect

We do not use tracking cookies, fingerprinting, or session recording.

We do not sell, rent, or share personal data with third-party advertisers. We have no advertisers.

## How we use it

- To reply to inquiries and schedule discovery calls.
- To run our AI agent and improve the prompts behind it, using anonymized aggregate patterns.
- To send the one-time AI Readiness Checklist follow-up email when you request it. We don't add you to a marketing list without explicit opt-in.
- To detect abuse and rate-limit automated traffic.

## Who we share it with

- **Anthropic** — for AI inference. Anthropic does not train models on API customer data.
- **Vercel** — for hosting. They process server logs as part of running the platform.
- **Supabase** — managed Postgres for our application data, including form submissions.
- **Resend** — if we have it enabled, for transactional email delivery.

Each of the above is bound by a Data Processing Agreement consistent with GDPR and India's DPDP Act.

## How long we keep it

Form submissions: as long as the inquiry is active, then at least 12 months for follow-up. Beyond that, we delete or anonymize.

AI conversations: 90 days at full fidelity, then anonymized and retained as aggregate metrics.

Server logs: 30 days.

## Your rights

You can request access to, correction of, export of, or deletion of your personal data. Email [privacy@aliansoftware.net](mailto:privacy@aliansoftware.net) and we'll respond within 30 days.

If you're in the EU/UK, you have rights under GDPR. If you're in India, you have rights under the DPDP Act. We honor both, regardless of where you're located.

## Children

The site is intended for business users. We don't knowingly collect personal data from anyone under 16. If you believe we have, please email us and we'll delete it.

## Changes

We may update this policy. Material changes get an entry in the [changelog](/changelog) and an updated "last updated" date above.

Questions about this document? [legal@aliansoftware.net](mailto:legal@aliansoftware.net) — we'll route to the right human.

# Let's talk.

Three ways to reach us — pick whichever feels lightest. We reply within one business day on every channel.

## Send us a message

We'll get back to you within one business day. Most replies come from someone you'd actually work with on the project.

Legal

# Terms of Service

Last updated: 14 May 2026

[Privacy](/legal/privacy)[Terms](/legal/terms)[Cookies](/legal/cookies)

These terms govern your use of the Alian AI website ("the site") and the AI tools accessible on it. Engagements with us are governed by a separate Master Service Agreement (MSA) and Statement of Work (SOW) — these terms only cover use of the public site.

## Acceptable use

You may use the site for ordinary business research, demos of our AI agents, and to contact us. You may not:

- Attempt to extract our prompts, system instructions, or training data
- Use the AI agents to produce content that's illegal, harassing, defamatory, or that infringes someone else's rights
- Probe, scan, scrape, or stress the site beyond ordinary use
- Impersonate any person or misrepresent your relationship with us

## The AI agents

The AI agents on the site (the homepage Live Problem Solver and the floating chat) are demos. Outputs are generated by large language models and may be incorrect. Don't make legal, medical, financial, or business decisions based solely on those outputs. Verify everything that matters with a human.

We rate-limit the agents per IP. If you hit the limit, that's working as intended.

## Your content

You retain ownership of anything you type into our forms or AI agents. By submitting it, you grant us a limited license to use it internally to reply to you and to improve our service. We don't publish your submissions without explicit permission.

## Our content

Case studies, blog posts, and site copy are © Alian Software. You may quote and link with attribution. Don't republish in full without permission.

Our brand assets (the Alian AI wordmark, the brand colors) are not available for use without permission.

## Warranties + liability

The site and its AI agents are provided "as is". To the maximum extent permitted by law, we disclaim all warranties — express or implied — including fitness for a particular purpose and non-infringement.

Our maximum liability for anything related to use of the site is limited to $100 USD or its local-currency equivalent. This does not limit liability for fraud or anything law doesn't allow us to disclaim.

## Governing law

These terms are governed by the laws of Maharashtra, India. Disputes are resolved by the courts of Pune. If you're not in India, you still agree to this — it keeps things simple for both of us.

## Changes

We may update these terms. Continued use of the site after an update means you accept the new terms. Material changes get logged in the [changelog](/changelog).

## Contact

Questions about these terms? [legal@aliansoftware.net](mailto:legal@aliansoftware.net)

Questions about this document? [legal@aliansoftware.net](mailto:legal@aliansoftware.net) — we'll route to the right human.

Tool

# Token counter.

Estimate how many tokens a prompt, document, or transcript will use. Approximation good enough for scoping. Runs in your browser — your text never leaves the page.

Try a sample:System prompt (short)Long doc (1 page)

Characters

0

Words

0

Tokens (est.)

0

Click to copy

Approximation only — uses 4 characters per token, which is reasonable for English. Real tokenizers vary by model. For exact counts, use the tokenizer from your model's SDK.

Handoff

# What you actually walk away with.

Every artifact we deliver when an engagement ships. Code in your repo from day one, prompts and configs you can edit, observability you can read, an eval suite that survives us leaving, and a runbook your on-call doesn't curse.

[Book a 20-min call →](/book-a-demo)[Read the methodology](/methodology)

## Six artifacts you get

- ### Code in your repo

Every commit lives in your GitHub / GitLab from week one. Branch protections, PR templates, CI configs — all yours. No private mirror, no 'we'll send the tarball.'
- ### Prompts, configs, agent definitions

Every system prompt, every agent config, every eval case in version-controlled YAML/JSON inside your repo. Editable by your team without us.
- ### Observability dashboards

Langfuse instance configured in your account, with the dashboards we used during the build. You see what we saw — daily ops becomes weekly review.
- ### Eval suite + replay infrastructure

20+ seeded cases, CI integration that fails merges on regressions, weekly replay job already scheduled. Named owner on your side.
- ### Runbook + on-call playbook

Written runbook for the agent: how to deploy, how to roll back, how to investigate a bad output, how to retrain. The thing your on-call wishes they had.
- ### Knowledge-transfer session

Two 90-minute working sessions with your team — one for engineers (architecture + ops), one for the business owner (eval review + drift signals). Recorded.

### What's transferred to you

- All source code, prompts, and configs — assigned in writing on engagement close
- All API keys, infrastructure accounts, observability accounts — provisioned in your name from day one
- All fine-tuned models (if any) — hosted on your account or exported as weights
- All datasets we created during the engagement — yours, with provenance documented
- All eval suite cases including the ones derived from production — yours

### What we keep

- The right to reference the engagement in our portfolio (with your sign-off on what's public vs. anonymized)
- Generic patterns we developed that don't contain your IP
- Aggregate metrics for our internal benchmarks (anonymized)

## What happens after handoff

Three patterns. Pick the one that fits your team — we don't push.

- ### Hands-off

You take it from here. We're available by email if something breaks, but no ongoing commitment.
- ### Hourly retainer

Paid as you use it. We respond within a business day for triage, longer for bigger work. Easy to ramp up or down.
- ### Monthly Retainer

Dedicated team allocation for ongoing tuning, new agent additions, eval coverage as the product evolves. Most of our flagship clients renew here.

## Want to see what handoff looks like?

We'll share a sanitized handoff packet from a past engagement on request — runbook structure, eval suite layout, the works.

[Request a sample packet](mailto:sales@aliansoftware.net?subject=Sample handoff packet)[See the methodology](/methodology)
